title: Living Space Ecology
subtitle: Cozy, minimalist, and managing home resources
description: This checklist covers the foundation of home organization: from decluttering and storage systems to managing home finances and creating a restorative atmosphere. Order in the home directly impacts order in the mind and daily energy levels.

items.title: Decluttering by Category
items.source: Marie Kondo — The Life-Changing Magic of Tidying Up
items.text: Visual noise from excess stuff quietly consumes cognitive resources. Cleaning by room (closet today, drawer tomorrow) doesn't work because you don't see the real volume of belongings. Only by gathering all clothes from all rooms in one pile can you realize the scale and make decisions.
items.tip: Start with the 'Clothing' category. Gather everything in the center of the room and keep only what you actually wear and love.

items.title: The 'One In, One Out' Principle
items.text: The number of things in a home tends to increase unless you introduce a conscious rule. Each new purchase should be accompanied by parting with one old item of similar function. This stops entropy and keeps space free.
items.tip: Bought a new mug — give away or throw out the old cracked one. Bought a new t-shirt — put one old one in the donation bag.

items.title: Storage System
items.source: David Allen — Getting Things Done (Home Edition)
items.text: Every item not in its place is an open loop in the brain. When an object has a single 'home', the brain stops spending energy searching for it and worrying. The storage system should be intuitive for all family members.
items.tip: Group items by frequency of use: daily items at eye and hand level, seasonal items on higher shelves.

items.title: The 5-Minute Rule
items.text: Clutter accumulates from postponed micro-actions. A cup in the sink, a jacket in the closet, wipe the counter — all take less than 5 minutes. The habit of doing these small tasks immediately, rather than 'later', saves hours on major cleaning and keeps the home in 'guest-ready' condition.
items.tip: If an action takes less than 5 minutes — do it right now, don't postpone.

items.title: Lighting Scenario
items.text: Lighting directly controls circadian rhythms and hormonal status. Bright cool light in the evening suppresses melatonin production and hinders sleep onset. Warm dim light signals the brain that night is approaching and promotes relaxation.
items.tip: Use multiple light sources in a room: overhead light for cleaning, floor lamps and sconces with warm spectrum (2700K) for evening.

items.title: Biophilia in Interior Design
items.text: Humans are evolutionarily programmed for contact with nature. The presence of live plants, natural textures of wood and stone, and views of greenery lower cortisol levels and accelerate recovery from stress. This is not decor, but an element of mental hygiene.
items.tip: Get at least one large, unpretentious plant (snake plant, ZZ plant) in the area where you spend the most time.

items.title: The 'One Load' Method
items.text: Perfectionism in cleaning leads to procrastination. The idea of cleaning 'the whole kitchen perfectly' is intimidating in scale. The 'One Load' or 'Zone' method suggests focusing on one small area at a time, but doing it regularly. The sink is the starting point of order.
items.tip: Start with a sparkling kitchen sink every evening. This is the anchor habit that triggers a chain reaction of order.

items.title: Home Accounting
items.source: Ramit Sethi — I Will Teach You To Be Rich
items.text: Daily life eats the budget through small but frequent expenses: takeaway coffee, spontaneous food delivery, forgotten subscriptions. Conscious weekly meal planning and a shopping list reduce food costs by 20-30% without sacrificing quality of life.
items.tip: Review the refrigerator before going to the store once a week. Make a list and stick to it.

items.title: Restoration Zone
items.source: Alain de Botton — The Architecture of Happiness
items.text: The home must have a place free from screens, work documents, and reminders of obligations. A corner with an armchair, blanket, and book where you can just 'be'. The presence of such a zone signals the psyche that it is safe and can relax.
items.tip: Create a 'calm altar': an armchair, soft light, a candle or aromatherapy lamp. Spend 10 minutes there daily without a phone.

items.title: Seasonal Review
items.text: Things tend to accumulate unnoticed. Four times a year (ideally with the change of seasons) do a light review of your wardrobe, kitchenware, and medicine cabinet. It takes a couple of hours but prevents chaos and allows timely disposal of expired and broken items.
items.tip: Set a calendar reminder for the first day of each season: 'Space Review'.

quiz.q: Why does Marie Kondo recommend tidying by category rather than by room?
a: To see the total volume of items in one category
To avoid walking around the whole house

quiz.q: What is the essence of the 'One In, One Out' principle?
a: Enter the house through only one door
New item — minus one old similar item
Buy only one item per day

quiz.q: What light spectrum is recommended for evening bedroom lighting?
What is an 'anchor habit' in the FlyLady system?
a: Mopping all floors in the house
A clean kitchen sink every evening

quiz.q: Why is a 'restoration zone' needed in the home?
a: To signal the psyche of safety and rest
